title: An aspirational perspective on the negative risk-return relationship
abstract: The existence of a negative risk-return relationship challenges the conventional wisdom of finance, which typically assumes a positive correlation between risk and return. Reference-dependent preferences, motivated by prospect theory, offer a possible explanation for this negative risk-return relation. However, as we demonstrate in this paper prospect theory does not provide a general explanation for this puzzle. We show that the expected utility theory with an aspiration level can effectively account for this phenomenon.
summary_en: A long-standing finance puzzle: across many assets, the risk-return relationship is negative — riskier assets often earn lower returns, contrary to standard theory. Prospect theory has been offered as an explanation. This paper shows prospect theory doesn't give a general account, but expected-utility theory with an aspiration level does. A cleaner, more tractable explanation for why observed risk-return relationships sometimes go the wrong way. Useful for portfolio-theory teaching and for thinking about retail-investor behaviour.
data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Motivated by empirical asset-pricing literature documenting the negative risk-return relationship.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian retail-investor protection (MNB, BÉT): the paper suggests that investor behaviour deviating from mean-variance optimisation can be captured by aspiration-level models. Relevant to MiFID II suitability assessments and to household-finance regulation. Caveat: theoretical; quantitative calibration to Hungarian household investment data would strengthen the policy case.
